title: Expose Your Local Web App to the Internet with Cloudflare Tunnel (Behind CGNAT / No Port Forwarding)
description: A complete step-by-step guide to exposing your locally-hosted web application (Next.js, React, Nginx, or any service) to the internet — without port forwarding, without a static public IP, and even behind CGNAT. Using Cloudflare Tunnel (cloudflared), you get HTTPS, DDoS protection, and a secure tunnel — all for free.
author: E-Learning Platform
heroAlt: Architecture diagram showing a local server with a web app connecting through Cloudflare Tunnel to the internet, bypassing ISP CGNAT and router port forwarding restrictions
tableOfContents: The Problem: Why Can't I Access My App from the Internet?
Step 7: Run as a System Service
Using Cloudflare Tunnel with Nginx (Reverse Proxy)

sections.title: The Problem: Why Can't I Access My App from the Internet?
sections.description: Understanding CGNAT, ISP restrictions, and why traditional port forwarding may not work.

items.label: The Traditional Approach
items.text:
When you host a web app on your local machine or a VM on your home network, the standard way to make it accessible from the internet is:

1. Get a public IP from your ISP
2. Set up port forwarding on your router (e.g., TP-Link Deco)
3. Allow the port through your firewall
4. Access your app at http://<public-ip>:<port>

This works fine — until it doesn't.

items.label: CGNAT (Carrier-Grade NAT)
text: Most ISPs in Bangladesh (and many other countries) use CGNAT (Carrier-Grade NAT). This means your router doesn't get a unique public IP — instead, hundreds of customers share one public IP. Your router gets a private IP like 100.x.x.x or 172.x.x.x.
With CGNAT, port forwarding is impossible. The ISP's router owns the public IP, not yours. Any incoming connection on port 80, 81, 3000, or any other port will never reach your machine.
How to check if you have CGNAT: Compare your public IP (what you see on https://api.ipify.org) with your router's WAN IP (check your TP-Link Deco app). If they're different, you're behind CGNAT.

rows: ISP shares one public IP among many customers
Cloudflare Tunnel, ngrok, or request a public IP from ISP
Some ISPs block ports 80, 443, 25 for residential connections
Use a non-standard port (e.g., 8080, 8443) or Cloudflare Tunnel
Your public IP changes every time the router reboots
DDNS service (like TP-Link Deco DDNS) or Cloudflare Tunnel
You don't have a fixed public IP address
Cloudflare Tunnel (no IP needed), DDNS, or ngrok
Some ISP-provided routers have limited or broken port forwarding
Cloudflare Tunnel, or replace the router

items.label: The Solution: Tunneling
items.text:
Instead of waiting for incoming connections (which requires an open port), tunneling works by having your machine initiate an outbound connection to a cloud service, which then forwards incoming requests back through that same connection. This bypasses NAT, CGNAT, firewalls, and port blocking — because outbound connections are always allowed.

Cloudflare Tunnel is the best free option. Let's set it up.

sections.title: What is Cloudflare Tunnel?
sections.description: Understanding how Cloudflare Tunnel works and why it's the best free tunneling solution.

items.label: How It Works
items.text:
Cloudflare Tunnel (previously named Argo Tunnel) creates an encrypted tunnel from your local machine to Cloudflare's edge network. Here's the flow:

1. You run a lightweight daemon called cloudflared on your machine
2. cloudflared establishes an outbound connection to Cloudflare's network
3. You configure a domain or subdomain in Cloudflare's DNS
4. When someone visits your domain, Cloudflare proxies the request through the tunnel to your local machine
5. No ports need to be open on your router. No public IP needed. No firewall changes needed.

items.label: Key Benefits
text: ✓ No port forwarding required — works behind any NAT, CGNAT, or firewall
✓ Free — unlimited tunnels, unlimited traffic, no monthly cost
✓ HTTPS automatically — Cloudflare provides SSL/TLS certificates
✓ DDoS protection — Cloudflare's network absorbs attacks
✓ No public IP needed — your app stays on your local network
✓ Encrypted end-to-end — traffic is encrypted between visitor and Cloudflare, and between Cloudflare and your machine
✓ Always-on — runs as a system service, auto-reconnects on failure
✓ Custom domains supported — use your own domain (e.g., app.yourdomain.com)
✓ Quick testing without a domain — TryCloudflare gives you a random URL instantly

items.label: Requirements
text: 1. A local web app running on your machine (Next.js, Nginx, React, etc.) — we'll use port 81 for this guide
2. A Linux server or VM (Ubuntu 22.04 recommended) — we'll install cloudflared on this
3. Internet connection — cloudflared needs outbound access to Cloudflare (ports 443, 7844, 80)
4. (Optional) A domain name managed in Cloudflare DNS — if you want a custom domain instead of a random URL
5. sudo/root access on the machine running cloudflared

items.label: Our Setup for This Guide
items.text:
Throughout this guide, we'll use this setup:

• Local web app: Next.js (or any app) running on a Linux VM at 192.168.68.200:81
• Host OS: Windows 11 with TP-Link Deco mesh router
• VM: Ubuntu 22.04 on VMware/VirtualBox (Bridged network mode)
• Cloudflare account: Free tier (sign up at https://dash.cloudflare.com/sign-up)

Note: This works identically whether your app runs on Windows, Linux, macOS, or a Raspberry Pi. The cloudflared daemon is available for all platforms.

sections.title: Step 1: Install cloudflared
sections.description: Install the Cloudflare Tunnel client on your machine.

items.label: On Linux (Ubuntu/Debian) — Recommended
items.code:
user@vm:~$ wget -q https://github.com/cloudflare/cloudflared/releases/latest/download/cloudflared-linux-amd64.deb
user@vm:~$ sudo dpkg -i cloudflared-linux-amd64.deb
(Reading database ... 123456 files and directories currently installed.)
Preparing to unpack cloudflared-linux-amd64.deb ...
Unpacking cloudflared (2025.12.0) ...
Setting up cloudflared (2025.12.0) ...

user@vm:~$ cloudflared version
cloudflared version 2025.12.0 (built 2025-12-10)

items.label: Verification
items.code:
user@vm:~$ which cloudflared
/usr/local/bin/cloudflared

user@vm:~$ cloudflared --version
cloudflared version 2025.12.0

subsections.title: Alternative: Install on Windows (PowerShell)
subsections.description: If you want to run cloudflared on Windows directly instead of the VM.

items.label: Install via winget
items.code:
PS C:\Users\NC> winget install cloudflare.cloudflared
Found cloudflared [cloudflare.cloudflared]
Downloading: https://github.com/cloudflare/cloudflared/releases/...
Successfully installed

items.label: Or download manually
items.code:
PS C:\Users\NC> curl.exe -L --output cloudflared.msi https://github.com/cloudflare/cloudflared/releases/latest/download/cloudflared-windows-amd64.msi
PS C:\Users\NC> msiexec /i cloudflared.msi

items.label: Verify installation
items.code:
PS C:\Users\NC> cloudflared version
cloudflared version 2025.12.0

sections.title: Step 2: Authenticate with Cloudflare
sections.description: Link the cloudflared client to your Cloudflare account.

items.label: Run the login command
items.code:
user@vm:~$ cloudflared tunnel login

A browser window should have opened at the following URL:

https://dash.cloudflare.com/argotunnel?callback=...

If the browser failed to open, open the URL above in your browser

2025-12-10T10:30:00Z INF Waiting for login...
2025-12-10T10:30:15Z INF You have successfully logged in!
2025-12-10T10:30:15Z INF A new certificate has been saved to /home/user/.cloudflared/cert.pem

items.label: What happens
items.text:
This opens a browser tab asking you to log in to Cloudflare and select a domain. After authentication:

• cloudflared saves a certificate at ~/.cloudflared/cert.pem
• This cert allows cloudflared to manage tunnels and DNS on your behalf
• You only need to do this once per machine

sections.title: Step 3: Create a Tunnel
sections.description: Create a named tunnel that will connect your local app to Cloudflare.

items.label: Create the tunnel
items.code:
user@vm:~$ cloudflared tunnel create my-app

Tunnel credentials written to /home/user/.cloudflared/my-app.json

cloudflared will choose the global region for this tunnel from the Cloudflare network.
You can configure your tunnel to run a specific service locally at any given hostname.
For more, see: https://developers.cloudflare.com/cloudflare-one/connections/connect-apps/install-and-setup/tunnel-guide/

Tunnel ID: abc12345-6789-def0-1234-56789abcdef0

items.label: Verify the tunnel exists
items.code:
user@vm:~$ cloudflared tunnel list

ID                                   Name    Created
abc12345-6789-def0-1234-56789abcdef0 my-app  2025-12-10T10:35:00Z

user@vm:~$ cat /home/user/.cloudflared/my-app.json
{
  "AccountTag": "your-account-id",
  "TunnelSecret": "base64-encoded-secret",
  "TunnelID": "abc12345-6789-def0-1234-56789abcdef0",
  "TunnelName": "my-app"
}

rows: Your API certificate — authenticates cloudflared with Cloudflare
Tunnel credentials — the secret key that proves your tunnel is genuine
Tunnel configuration — defines which local services to proxy (we'll create this next)

sections.title: Step 4: Configure the Tunnel
sections.description: Tell Cloudflare Tunnel which local service to forward traffic to.

items.label: Config file content
items.code:
user@vm:~$ nano ~/.cloudflared/config.yml

# Paste the following (replace with your tunnel ID):

tunnel: abc12345-6789-def0-1234-56789abcdef0
credentials-file: /home/user/.cloudflared/abc12345-6789-def0-1234-56789abcdef0.json

# Route traffic based on hostname or path
# If you have a custom domain:
igress:
  - hostname: app.yourdomain.com
    service: http://localhost:81
  # Catch-all rule - must be last
  - service: http_status:404

items.label: Understanding ingress rules
items.text:
The ingress section defines how to route incoming requests:

• hostname: Match requests for a specific domain (e.g., app.yourdomain.com)
• service: The local address to forward requests to (http://localhost:81, http://192.168.68.200:3000, etc.)
• Path prefix: You can also match by path (e.g., hostname: "*.yourdomain.com" path: "/api/*")
• The last rule must be a catch-all (no hostname) — usually http_status:404 or http_status:403

If you don't have a custom domain yet, skip the hostname and use the quick test method in Step 6.

subsections.title: Multiple services example
subsections.description: If you have multiple apps running on different ports:

items.label: Multi-app config
items.code:
tunnel: abc12345-6789-def0-1234-56789abcdef0
credentials-file: /home/user/.cloudflared/abc12345-6789-def0-1234-56789abcdef0.json

ingress:
  - hostname: app.yourdomain.com
    service: http://localhost:3000
  - hostname: api.yourdomain.com
    service: http://localhost:4000
  - hostname: blog.yourdomain.com
    service: http://localhost:81
  - hostname: "*.yourdomain.com"
    service: http://localhost:8080
  - service: http_status:404

sections.title: Step 5: Run the Tunnel
sections.description: Start the tunnel and verify it connects successfully.

items.label: Start the tunnel
items.code:
user@vm:~$ cloudflared tunnel run my-app

2025-12-10T10:40:00Z INF Starting tunnel tunnelID=abc12345-6789-def0-1234-56789abcdef0
2025-12-10T10:40:00Z INF Connection 0 registered connIndex=0
2025-12-10T10:40:00Z INF Connection 1 registered connIndex=1
2025-12-10T10:40:00Z INF Connection 2 registered connIndex=2
2025-12-10T10:40:00Z INF Connection 3 registered connIndex=3

items.label: What you should see
items.text:
If you see 4 connections registered (Connection 0-3), the tunnel is up and running. These are 4 redundant connections to Cloudflare's edge — if one drops, traffic seamlessly shifts to another.

Keep this terminal open. If you close it, the tunnel stops. We'll set it up as a service later.

items.label: Avoid this common error
items.code:
ERR Unable to connect to the local service: dial tcp 127.0.0.1:81: connect: connection refused

# This means your local service (e.g., Nginx on port 81) isn't running:
user@vm:~$ sudo systemctl status nginx
● nginx.service - A high-performance web server and reverse proxy server
     Loaded: loaded
     Active: inactive (dead)

# Start it:
user@vm:~$ sudo systemctl start nginx
user@vm:~$ sudo systemctl status nginx
     Active: active (running)

sections.title: Step 6: Set Up DNS (Custom Domain)
sections.description: Configure your domain to route traffic through the Cloudflare Tunnel.

items.label: Route DNS through the tunnel
items.code:
user@vm:~$ cloudflared tunnel route dns my-app app.yourdomain.com

2025-12-10T10:45:00Z INF Added CNAME record app.yourdomain.com → abc12345-6789-def0-1234-56789abcdef0.cfargotunnel.com

items.label: What this does
text:
This creates a CNAME record in Cloudflare DNS:
• app.yourdomain.com → abc12345-6789-def0-1234-56789abcdef0.cfargotunnel.com
• The proxy (orange cloud) is automatically enabled
• Traffic to app.yourdomain.com now flows through Cloudflare → your tunnel → localhost:81

You can also do this manually in the Cloudflare Dashboard:
1. Go to Cloudflare Dashboard → your domain → DNS
2. Add a CNAME record:
   - Name: app
   - Target: abc12345-6789-def0-1234-56789abcdef0.cfargotunnel.com
   - Proxy status: Proxied (orange cloud)
3. Click Save

items.label: Verify DNS propagation
items.code:
user@vm:~$ dig app.yourdomain.com +short
abc12345-6789-def0-1234-56789abcdef0.cfargotunnel.com

# Or on Windows:
PS C:\Users\NC> nslookup app.yourdomain.com
Server:  ...
Name:    app.yourdomain.com
Address: 104.16.x.x  (Cloudflare IP)

sections.title: Step 7: Run as a System Service
sections.description: Make the tunnel start automatically on boot and stay running permanently.

items.label: Install as a systemd service
items.code:
user@vm:~$ sudo cloudflared service install

2025-12-10T10:50:00Z INF Installing systemd service for cloudflared
2025-12-10T10:50:00Z INF Successfully installed as systemd service

user@vm:~$ sudo systemctl start cloudflared
user@vm:~$ sudo systemctl enable cloudflared
Created symlink /etc/systemd/system/multi-user.target.wants/cloudflared.service → /etc/systemd/system/cloudflared.service

items.label: Check service status
items.code:
user@vm:~$ sudo systemctl status cloudflared
● cloudflared.service - cloudflared
     Loaded: loaded (/etc/systemd/system/cloudflared.service; enabled; vendor preset: enabled)
     Active: active (running) since Wed 2025-12-10 10:50:30 BST; 1min ago
   Main PID: 12345 (cloudflared)
      Tasks: 10 (limit: 12345)
     Memory: 15.2M
        CPU: 0.456s
     CGroup: /system.slice/cloudflared.service
             └─12345 /usr/local/bin/cloudflared tunnel run

items.label: Service management commands
items.code:
user@vm:~$ sudo systemctl stop cloudflared    # Stop the tunnel
user@vm:~$ sudo systemctl start cloudflared     # Start the tunnel
user@vm:~$ sudo systemctl restart cloudflared   # Restart the tunnel
user@vm:~$ sudo systemctl disable cloudflared   # Disable auto-start
user@vm:~$ sudo journalctl -u cloudflared -f    # View live logs

subsections.title: Alternative: Windows Service
subsections.description: If you installed cloudflared on Windows:

items.label: Install as Windows service
items.code:
PS C:\Users\NC> cloudflared service install
PS C:\Users\NC> Start-Service cloudflared
PS C:\Users\NC> Set-Service cloudflared -StartupType Automatic

# Verify:
PS C:\Users\NC> Get-Service cloudflared

Status   Name               DisplayName
------   ----               -----------
Running  cloudflared        cloudflared

sections.title: Quick Test Without a Domain (TryCloudflare)
sections.description: Test your tunnel immediately using a temporary Cloudflare URL — no domain needed.

items.label: Run a one-off tunnel
items.code:
user@vm:~$ cloudflared tunnel --url http://localhost:81

2025-12-10T11:00:00Z INF Requesting new quick tunnel on trycloudflare.com...
2025-12-10T11:00:05Z INF Your quick Tunnel has been created! Visit it at:
    https://random-words-1234.trycloudflare.com
2025-12-10T11:00:05Z INF Cannot determine default ingress from the URL, using default
2025-12-10T11:00:05Z INF Connection 0 registered connIndex=0
2025-12-10T11:00:05Z INF Connection 1 registered connIndex=1
2025-12-10T11:00:05Z INF Connection 2 registered connIndex=2
2025-12-10T11:00:05Z INF Connection 3 registered connIndex=3

items.label: Test it
items.text:
Open the URL (e.g., https://random-words-1234.trycloudflare.com) in any browser — on your phone (mobile data), a friend's computer, anywhere in the world.

Your local web app at port 81 is now accessible globally. No router config, no port forwarding, no firewall changes.

Note: TryCloudflare URLs are temporary — they last as long as the tunnel process runs. They're great for testing, demos, and short-term sharing.

sections.title: Using Cloudflare Tunnel with Nginx (Reverse Proxy)
sections.description: How to configure Cloudflare Tunnel when Nginx is running as a reverse proxy on your VM.

items.label: Common Nginx + Cloudflare Tunnel Setup
items.text: Many users run Nginx as a reverse proxy on port 80/81, which in turn proxies to a Next.js app (or other service) on a different port. In this case, point cloudflared to Nginx's port (81).

items.label: Nginx config example
Restart Nginx
items.code:
user@vm:~$ sudo nginx -t
nginx: the configuration file /etc/nginx/nginx.conf syntax is ok
nginx: configuration file /etc/nginx/nginx.conf test is successful

user@vm:~$ sudo systemctl restart nginx
user@vm:~$ sudo systemctl status nginx
● nginx.service - A high-performance web server and reverse proxy server
     Loaded: loaded
     Active: active (running)

# Now point cloudflared to Nginx:
user@vm:~$ cloudflared tunnel run --url http://localhost:81
# Or in config.yml:
# service: http://localhost:81

items.label: Performance tip
items.text: With Cloudflare Tunnel, you can also disable direct port access (set Nginx to listen only on 127.0.0.1:81 instead of 0.0.0.0:81) — because all traffic comes through the tunnel. This improves security by ensuring your app is only accessible via Cloudflare.

sections.title: Troubleshooting
sections.description: Common issues and how to fix them.

items.label: Issue 1: Connection refused — local service not running
items.code:
ERR Unable to connect to the local service: dial tcp 127.0.0.1:81: connect: connection refused

# Fix: Make sure your web app (Nginx, Next.js, etc.) is running and listening on the correct port.
user@vm:~$ sudo ss -tlnp | grep :81
LISTEN 0      511         127.0.0.1:81       0.0.0.0:*    users:(("nginx",pid=1234,fd=6))

# If nothing shows, start the service:
user@vm:~$ sudo systemctl start nginx

items.label: Issue 2: Cloudflare shows 521/522/523 errors
items.text:
These errors mean Cloudflare can't reach your tunnel:

• 521: Web server is down — your cloudflared isn't running
• 522: Connection timed out — cloudflared can't connect to Cloudflare's edge (check your internet)
• 523: Origin is unreachable — same as 522, network issue
• 524: A timeout occurred — your app is slow; increase timeouts

Fix: Check that cloudflared is running: sudo systemctl status cloudflared. Check your internet connection.

items.label: Issue 3: DNS not propagating
items.code:
user@vm:~$ dig app.yourdomain.com +short
# (no output) or returns an old IP

# Fix: Ensure the CNAME points to the tunnel ID:
cloudflared tunnel route dns my-app app.yourdomain.com

# Wait a few minutes for DNS propagation
# Verify the tunnel ID:
user@vm:~$ cloudflared tunnel info my-app
NAME     ID                                   STATUS
my-app   abc12345-6789-def0-1234-56789abcdef0 active

# The CNAME target should be:
# abc12345-6789-def0-1234-56789abcdef0.cfargotunnel.com

items.label: Issue 4: Can't access from mobile/outside
items.code:
# If you get ERR_CONNECTION_REFUSED or the page doesn't load:

# Test your tunnel is running:
user@vm:~$ curl -I http://localhost:81
HTTP/1.1 200 OK

# Test your tunnel URL from inside the VM:
user@vm:~$ curl -I https://app.yourdomain.com
HTTP/2 200

# If the tunnel isn't running, start it:
user@vm:~$ sudo systemctl restart cloudflared
user@vm:~$ sudo journalctl -u cloudflared -n 20 --no-pager

items.label: Issue 5: Want to check logs in real-time
items.code:
user@vm:~$ sudo journalctl -u cloudflared -f

# Or run cloudflared manually in debug mode:
user@vm:~$ cloudflared tunnel run my-app --loglevel debug

# This shows every request coming through the tunnel:
2025-12-10T11:10:00Z DBG GET https://app.yourdomain.com/ -> 200 in 15ms

items.label: Issue 6: Too many authentication failures
items.code:
# If you get auth errors, the credentials file may be stale:

# Delete and recreate the tunnel:
user@vm:~$ cloudflared tunnel delete my-app
user@vm:~$ rm ~/.cloudflared/my-app.json
user@vm:~$ cloudflared tunnel create my-app

# Update your config.yml with the new tunnel ID
# Then route DNS again:
user@vm:~$ cloudflared tunnel route dns my-app app.yourdomain.com

sections.title: Security Best Practices
sections.description: Keep your tunnel and application secure.

items.label: 1. Make your app listen only on localhost
items.text:
Since all traffic comes through the tunnel, your app doesn't need to listen on 0.0.0.0. Bind it to localhost only:

• Nginx: listen 127.0.0.1:81;
• Next.js: Not directly possible in dev mode, but in production with Nginx reverse proxy, bind Nginx to 127.0.0.1
• This way, even if someone on your local network knows the port, they can't access your app directly

items.label: 2. Use Cloudflare Access for authentication
items.text:
You can add an authentication layer using Cloudflare Access (free up to 50 users). This requires anyone visiting your domain to log in via Google, GitHub, email OTP, or other providers before reaching your app.

Cloudflare Dashboard → Zero Trust → Access → Applications → Add an application

items.label: 3. Restrict the tunnel with firewall rules
items.text:
You can add firewall rules on the VM to only allow traffic from Cloudflare's IP ranges:

user@vm:~$ sudo ufw allow from 173.245.48.0/20 to any port 81
user@vm:~$ sudo ufw allow from 103.21.244.0/22 to any port 81
user@vm:~$ sudo ufw deny 81

(Cloudflare IP ranges: https://www.cloudflare.com/ips/)

items.label: 4. Keep cloudflared updated
items.text:
Regularly update cloudflared for security patches:

user@vm:~$ sudo dpkg -i cloudflared-linux-amd64.deb
# Or auto-update with the install script:
user@vm:~$ curl -L https://github.com/cloudflare/cloudflared/releases/latest/download/cloudflared-linux-amd64.deb -o cloudflared.deb && sudo dpkg -i cloudflared.deb

items.label: 5. Rotate tunnel secrets periodically
items.text:
If you suspect a tunnel credential leak, delete the tunnel and recreate it:

user@vm:~$ cloudflared tunnel delete my-app
user@vm:~$ cloudflared tunnel create my-app
user@vm:~$ cloudflared tunnel route dns my-app app.yourdomain.com
# Update config.yml with new tunnel ID
user@vm:~$ sudo systemctl restart cloudflared

items.label: What You Learned
text: ✓ CGNAT prevents port forwarding — tunneling is the solution
✓ Cloudflare Tunnel is free, secure, and works on any network
✓ cloudflared creates an outbound tunnel from your machine to Cloudflare's edge
✓ No open ports needed on your router — the tunnel initiates the connection
✓ HTTPS and DDoS protection included for free
✓ You can use TryCloudflare for quick testing without a domain
✓ The tunnel runs as a background service for permanent availability
✓ Works with Nginx reverse proxy, Next.js, or any HTTP service
✓ Extra security with Cloudflare Access, localhost binding, and firewall rules

items.label: Quick Start Recap (5 commands)
items.code:
# 1. Install
sudo dpkg -i cloudflared-linux-amd64.deb

# 2. Login
cloudflared tunnel login

# 3. Create tunnel
cloudflared tunnel create my-app

# 4. Quick test (no domain needed)
cloudflared tunnel --url http://localhost:81

# 5. Permanent setup
cloudflared tunnel route dns my-app app.yourdomain.com
sudo cloudflared service install
sudo systemctl enable cloudflared
sudo systemctl start cloudflared

items.label: Next Steps
text: 1. Get a domain name (e.g., from Cloudflare Registrar, Namecheap, or GoDaddy) and add it to Cloudflare DNS
2. Set up Cloudflare Access (Zero Trust) for authentication
3. Configure Web Analytics in Cloudflare Dashboard for visitor insights
4. Set up a WAF rule to block malicious traffic
5. If you have multiple apps, create multiple tunnels or use ingress rules
6. Consider adding a caching layer (Cloudflare Cache or Nginx caching) for better performance
